item.title: Iduhu criticises Government focus on Chiefs
item.description: DEPUTY Opposition Leader Keith Iduhu has criticised the Government for focusing on rugby league instead of urgent national issues. Iduhu said the country was being distracted by “sideshows” while real problems such as the cost of living, rising prices, jobs, and election preparation were ignored.
item.content:encoded:
DEPUTY Opposition Leader Keith Iduhu has criticised the Government for focusing on rugby league instead of urgent national issues.

Iduhu said the country was being distracted by “sideshows” while real problems such as the cost of living, rising prices, jobs, and election preparation were ignored.

“How does the focus on rugby league and a “chief’s intervention in 2028” relate to preparing for the national election or improving people’s lives?” he said.

Iduhu said preparation for the General Election 2027 (GE27) was the most important task right now.

“In the 2026 budget, police have been allocated about K519 million, with K194 million set aside for elections,” he said.

“Has this money actually been released? If it hasn’t been released, then it has to be fast-tracked so that preparations can begin properly.”

Iduhu also raised concerns over the Electoral Commission, which has a budget of about K404 million, but has received only K50 million, even with the GE27 less than 11 months away.

He said that the delay was dangerous for democracy and showed poor planning.

“Elections cannot be treated lightly, as they determine the country’s leadership and future.”

Iduhu also questioned reports of large government fund releases without clear explanation, calling for transparency from the Finance minister on where the money was going.

“Like the reported K20 million linked to revenue from hiring out Defence facilities. This must be checked by the Auditor General and properly accounted for under law.”

Iduhu said the Government must stop focusing on rugby league and started focusing on free, fair, and safe election, and the cost-of-living pressure facing the people.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: No criminal intent behind K20 million transfer, says Marape
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape says that “initial information” available to him indicated no criminal intent behind the transfer of K20 million from the Defence Force trust fund to the National Development Bank. “It is my considered view that no ill intention is meant in this transaction. However, every government fund must have a clear and lawful purpose, and we will ensure that all processes are pr
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape says that “initial information” available to him indicated no criminal intent behind the transfer of K20 million from the Defence Force trust fund to the National Development Bank.

“It is my considered view that no ill intention is meant in this transaction. However, every government fund must have a clear and lawful purpose, and we will ensure that all processes are properly accounted for,” he said in a statement.

Marape, who is chairman of the Defence Council, said he only became aware of the council’s March 31 decision through social media.

He said that he “moved swiftly to initiate formal review of the decision”.

He welcomed public scrutiny on the K20 million transaction which was confirmed by two council members – Defence Secretary John Akipe and Defence Force Chief Rear-Admiral Philip Polewara – on Tuesday.

Marape cautioned against premature and misleading conclusions drawn without full facts.

Responding to concerns raised by Opposition Leader James Nomane, Marape said “issues of this nature play an important role in strengthening accountability” in Government.

“In a democracy, we must allow space for concerns to be raised, not just by the Opposition, but by any citizen or public official who believes something may not be right. These matters must then be tested through proper processes and institutions,” he said.

He warned that such concerns must be handled responsibly and verified through established legal and institutional mechanisms, rather than being prematurely labelled as criminal conduct.

“I do not tolerate graft and corruption. I do not tolerate illegal practices. We expect everyone to do the right thing,” he said.

 “Any suspicious or questionable transaction must be subjected to proper scrutiny through our Institutions, including Independent Commission Against Corruption, the Ombudsman Commission, and other relevant Authorities. These bodies exist to ensure accountability, and we will allow them to do their work.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: ‘Poison’ confirmed in fish deaths
item.description: INITIAL test results on dead marine life from Kafkaf in New Ireland have confirmed the presence of poisonous minerals in the samples taken. Fisheries and Marine Resources Minister Jelta Wong announced the results, saying risk assessment, including laboratory test results from the Singapore-based ALS Technichem PTE Limited on fish mortality in the villages along the New Ireland coast, showed traces
item.content:encoded:
INITIAL test results on dead marine life from Kafkaf in New Ireland have confirmed the presence of poisonous minerals in the samples taken.

Fisheries and Marine Resources Minister Jelta Wong announced the results, saying risk assessment, including laboratory test results from the Singapore-based ALS Technichem PTE Limited on fish mortality in the villages along the New Ireland coast, showed traces of arsenic, cadmium, chromium and lead.

All these minerals, while present in certain concentrations in the natural environment, can be life-threatening when concentrated.

The National Fisheries Authority commissioned a risk assessment investigation in February following reports of fish deaths and disturbance in water quality in and around Larairu lagoon in Kavieng, New Ireland on Dec 11, last year.

The laboratory test results conclude that:

Wong stressed the need for precautionary management of fish harvesting and consumption until repeat investigations and confirmatory risk assessment are completed.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Renewed fish deaths in New Ireland spark concern
item.description: FISH have started dying again in large numbers in the Tikana Local Level Government of New Ireland. There was a brief lull before fish deaths were again discovered two weeks ago.
item.content:encoded:
FISH have started dying again in large numbers in the Tikana Local Level Government of New Ireland.

There was a brief lull before fish deaths were again discovered two weeks ago.

A large number was found along the coast of Madina.

A team from local non-government organisation Ailan Awareness (AA) has been monitoring the situation.

It also received reports from two new locations – Lovongai Islands and Luatul village in Kavieng.

In West Lovongai, a dead dolphin was found.

Dead lobsters and eels were reported in Luatul.

Local chief and AA founder John Aini said most of the dead fish had “blood clots” in their eyes.

Cases of skin infections from contact with the sea are being reported, although they have slowed down in most affected areas.

Despite restrictions, some locals are still catching and eating fish.

So far, there have been no reports of people becoming ill.

“Without clear information from authorities like Fisheries and Conservation and Environment Protection Authority (Cepa) confirming the sea is safe, it puts pressure on them.”

Meanwhile, results from the Cepa have not yet been released.

During a meeting held last week by the New Ireland technical working group, officials explained that proper procedures would be followed before the report was made public.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Socceroo legend coming
item.description: FORMER Olympian and Socceroo icon Paul Wade will be in Port Moresby on May 30 as guest speaker on the night when 12 of Papua New Guinea’s sports athletes, organisations and individuals will be recognised for their contributions to various codes last year. Thirty-three finalists for the 34th SP Sports Awards, from 261 nominations, were announced in Port Moresby yesterday with 181 submitted online a
item.content:encoded:
FORMER Olympian and Socceroo icon Paul Wade will be in Port Moresby on May 30 as guest speaker on the night when 12 of Papua New Guinea’s sports athletes, organisations and individuals will be recognised for their contributions to various codes last year.

Thirty-three finalists for the 34th SP Sports Awards, from 261 nominations, were announced in Port Moresby yesterday with 181 submitted online and 80 in hard copy. The Junior Female Athlete of the Year category attracted the highest number of nominations at 30.

Announcing Wade’s acceptance of the invitation to address the awards night, SP Brewery corporate affairs manager John Nilkare said the Socceroo’s outstanding sporting career, leadership and commitment to community development makes him an exceptional choice and “his presence will inspire our sporting community and add great value to the nation’s premier celebration of sporting excellence”.

Wade said through a video recording that he is looking forward to meet everyone on May 30 with their great stories of success while, at the same time, sharing some of his own stories with the highs, the lows, the challenges and the successes.

Wade is the seventh most capped Socceroo and is regarded as one of the most influential captains in the history of Australian soccer, who is a driving force in the midfield position.

Wade led the Australian national soccer team for six consecutive years (1990-96).

Chairman of selectors Warwick Vele said of the nominations: “The growth in online submissions reflects increased public awareness and greater confidence in the process (including the engagement of a transparent auditing process to ensure full compliance and integrity).”

He said the high number of nominations in the Junior Female Athlete of the Year category “highlight both the depth of emerging talent and the high level of competition among the juniors”.

Vele said this shows the strong growth of women’s sport in the country.

Apart from addressing the awards night, the former Socceroo would also conduct a coaching clinic in Port Moresby alongside PNG Football Association officials on May 29.

Nilkare added: “His (Wade) insights and experience will inspire athletes and contribute to the continued growth and development of football (soccer) in PNG.”

His club career in Australia’s National Soccer League spanned more than 300 appearances, including championship success with Brunswick Juventus and South Melbourne FC.

At international level, he represented Australia at the 1988 Seoul Olympic Games and competed in two Fifa World Cup qualification campaigns in 1990 and 1994.

• REPRESENTED Australia 118 times between 1986 and 1996, including 84 full “A” internationals;

• TWO NSL championships: 1985 with Brunswick Juventus and 1991 with South Melbourne FC ;

• NSL Cup winner with South Melbourne FC (1990);

• NAMED NSL Player-of-the-Year in 1988; and,

• KEY contributor to South Melbourne FC’s 1993 minor premiership.

Since retiring from professional football, Wade continued to make a significant impact as a motivational speaker, coach educator and mentor.

His personal journey has inspired many across Australia and the Pacific.  Wade’s visit will include community engagement activities in partnership with the PNGFA.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Safety concerns dismissed
item.description: KERA Peters, the wife of Papua New Guinea Chiefs inaugural head coach Willie Peters, has dismissed safety concerns in Port Moresby after arriving in the country on Tuesday. “Not at all. Just coming off the plane this morning, I felt safe,” she said.
item.content:encoded:
KERA Peters, the wife of Papua New Guinea Chiefs inaugural head coach Willie Peters, has dismissed safety concerns in Port Moresby after arriving in the country on Tuesday.

“Not at all. Just coming off the plane this morning, I felt safe,” she said.

Concerns over safety for players and their families linked to the Chiefs project have been described as “blown out of proportion” by Chiefs boss Lorna McPherson after the coach’s family experienced life in Port Moresby first-hand.

“I think security is something we always need to take precautions with,” she said.

“But do I worry about what I do as an individual? No.

The 19th NRL expansion side continues to take shape, with early fears about security being challenged by those now arriving in the country.

During a media tour with Nine News , Kera Peters visited key club facilities.

Fresh from three flights from the United Kingdom, Kera and her son Jaxon were given their first look at what will become their new home base in Port Moresby.

Despite the long journey, the family appeared excited about the move and what the project represents.

“Oh, it’ll be amazing. For everyone. I think you can make such a difference to a country,” Kera said.

The family was taken on a guided tour of the Airways Hotel by McPherson, showcasing facilities that will support players and staff.

These include a swimming pool, day spa, hair salon, tennis court, gym, restaurants, and a children’s playground.

Jaxon Peters gave a simple reaction to the experience.

The tour also highlighted access to the exclusive Loloata Island Resort, which will be available for Chiefs personnel.

“Yeah, private island, it’s pretty special,” she said.

Construction is continuing on a major residential development for the club’s future players and officials.

By November next year, 67 purpose-built apartments will be completed, designed to house around 300 Chiefs staff and officials.

Property developer George Constantinou described the project as more than just housing, calling it a “whole-of-life village” with open space, retail areas, a large convenience store, and a medical centre.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Gulf Isou academy make perfect start in NYC
item.description: THE Gulf Isou Academy has made a perfect start in their inaugural season in the Papua New Guinea Rugby Football League (PNGRFL) development system, with strong early performances across all three divisions. Gulf Isou Under-19 coach Timothy Haroharo said the results so far have exceeded expectations, especially given that this is the academy’s first season in the structured pathway system.
item.content:encoded:
THE Gulf Isou Academy has made a perfect start in their inaugural season in the Papua New Guinea Rugby Football League (PNGRFL) development system, with strong early performances across all three divisions.

Gulf Isou Under-19 coach Timothy Haroharo said the results so far have exceeded expectations, especially given that this is the academy’s first season in the structured pathway system.

“This is our first year coming into the National Youth Competition (NYC) and the PNGRFL Academy system,” Haroharo told The National.

“Despite it being our inaugural year, the results have been really good so far in all three divisions. I am very impressed.”

He credited the PNGRFL and its development partners for establishing a proper system that is helping young players grow faster and more professionally.

“Now that we have a proper system in place, thanks to the PNGRFL and everyone working behind the scenes, our kids can learn the best from these structures,” he said.

“It also helps make our job easier as coaches.”

The Gulf Academy coach said there was a strong pool of raw talent in the province that now has a clear pathway to higher levels of rugby league.

He explained that the academy was focused on identifying, nurturing, and promoting both male and female players from the grassroots level.

“We have a pool of talent back home that needs an avenue to showcase their abilities,” he said.

“From there, they can be selected and developed further.”

Haroharo said this was only the beginning, with more players expected to be brought into the system in the future.

“We are working to get the best players, both male and female, to nurture them. This is just the first crop. We will keep looking for more talent.”

While early wins are encouraging, Haroharo stressed that the competition was not only about winning matches, but about building discipline and long-term player development.

“Being part of the competition is not just about winning games,” he said. “It is about instilling discipline and seeing who can progress. Everything good starts from doing the simple things right at the ground level.”

Haroharo also praised the coaching staff across all three divisions, saying teamwork had been a major factor in the academy’s early progress.

“Huge credit to all the coaching staff. We are all working together to get the best results from these kids,” he said.

“This is just the beginning for Gulf Academy. There is more to come.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: 34th edition of SP Sports Awards set for May 30
item.description: THE 2026 SP Sports Awards’ 34th edition is set for May 30 at the Crown Hotel in Port Moresby with 33 finalists announced yesterday at the Sir John Guise Stadium. Following an impressive level of interest and participation in the nomination process, only 33 nominees made the final list out of 261 nominations that went in across 12 categories.
item.content:encoded:
THE 2026 SP Sports Awards’ 34th edition is set for May 30 at the Crown Hotel in Port Moresby with 33 finalists announced yesterday at the Sir John Guise Stadium.

Following an impressive level of interest and participation in the nomination process, only 33 nominees made the final list out of 261 nominations that went in across 12 categories.

SP Sports Awards selection panel chairman Warwick Vele said the growth in online submissions reflected increased public awareness of the SP Sports Awards and greater confidence in the online nomination process.

“This increase shows that the SP Sports Awards continue to be recognised as a trusted and meaningful platform for celebrating sporting excellence in PNG,” he said.

Vele added the vetting and selection process was conducted with the highest level of transparency and integrity where the final vetting of all finalists was independently conducted by KPMG PNG. 

Male athlete of the year – Timothy Tove Tuna (Athletics), Pais Wisil (Athletics), and Morea Baru (Weightlifting).

Female athlete of the year – Dika Toua (Weightlifting), Adrine Monagi (Athletics), and Ramona Padio (Soccer).

Carbine Club: Team of the year – PNG Bilums (Soccer), PNG Lewas (Cricket) and PNG Amoa (Volleyball).

Brands Pacific: National performance of the year – On Track Tribes Touch Footy (Touch Footy), Gulf Komara FC (Soccer), and Sparrows Netball Club (Netball).

Community Sports Initiative – Pilai Senis AFL (AFL), Jonah Kautu Academy (Rugby), and Sugu Peace Basketball (Basketball).

Coca-Cola Europacific Partners PNG: Junior male athlete of the year – Lawrence Reva Oala (Weightlifting), Leo Aisi (Volleyball), and River-Jordan Hakena (Tennis).

Coca-Cola Europacific Partners PNG: Junior female athlete of the year – Jhaynali Tokome-Garap (Swimming), Ani Geua Gavera (Weightlifting), and Timonaliz Alarcos Kriebisch (Squash).

CPL: Para-athlete of the year – Morea Mararos (Shot Put), Jerome Bunge (Athletics), and Dorna Longbut (Athletics and Badminton).

Trukai Industries Limited: Sports official of the year – Mark Keto (Boxing), Willie Mavara Tamasi (Weightlifting), and Nelson Stone (Multi-Sport).

Trukai Industries Limited: Sports Media of the year – Jamie Haro (Freelance), Isaac Liri (NBC), and Terry Longbut (TVWan).

Sports photo of the year – Gairo Varo and Mum – Gima Ila, Boys – Michael Tamty Pais, and Softball – Simon Keslep.

The announcement of finalists also marked the opening of public voting for the 12th category – People’s Choice Award, where the winner will be determined by the highest number of votes from the public.

Finalists eligible for the People’s Choice Awards include four categories: male athlete of the year, female athlete of the year, team of the year, and national performance of the year.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Welcome home Alex Johnston
item.description: THE news that Alex Johnston has signed with the Papua New Guinea Chiefs is the biggest boost our new club could ask for. As Michael Chammas said, you’re the greatest try scorer in our game and a Papua New Guinean icon.
item.content:encoded:
THE news that Alex Johnston has signed with the Papua New Guinea Chiefs is the biggest boost our new club could ask for.

As Michael Chammas said, you’re the greatest try scorer in our game and a Papua New Guinean icon.

To see both in one person, wearing a Chiefs jersey in 2028, is a dream come true.

Born in Lae and raised on footy, you’ve carved out an National Rugby League (NRL) career that will be remembered for generations, always proudly wearing the Kumuls jersey.

You have long spoken about wanting an NRL team in PNG, and now you’re not just supporting it –you’re leading it from the wing.

With 190-plus NRL tries, a premiership with the South Sydney Rabbitohs, and professionalism that turns training into winning, the standards you bring will shape this club for years to come.

Beyond the tryline, kids in Goroka, Alotau, and Buka will see you in Chiefs colours at our own NRL ground, making their dreams real.

You’ll mentor young players, bridging the Digicel Cup and NRL pathways.

Alex, you’ve always carried PNG with you.

You did not have to come – you chose to.

Thank you for believing in this vision and coming home.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Governing by hollow ‘condemning’
item.description: IT seems that the word ‘condemning’ has become a staple in everyday conversation by those in authority. Every time there’s an issue – be it corruption, mismanagement, or injustice – our leaders, and the public alike are quick to ‘condemn’ it.
item.content:encoded:
IT seems that the word ‘condemning’ has become a staple in everyday conversation by those in authority.

Every time there’s an issue – be it corruption, mismanagement, or injustice – our leaders, and the public alike are quick to ‘condemn’ it.

Yet, despite the endless rounds of condemnation, nothing ever changes.

We condemn corruption, but it continues. We condemn violence, yet it persists.

We condemn poor leadership, but the same faces remain in power.

It’s as if the act of condemning has become a hollow ritual, a convenient way to show outrage without any real consequence or follow-through.

While it’s important to speak out against what’s wrong, the constant use of condemnation without concrete action only adds to the sense of powerlessness that many of us feel.

Condemnation without change is just noise.  If we are serious about improving our nation, we need more than words; we need real, tangible actions that challenge the status quo.

It’s time we stop condemning for the sake of condemning.

If we want to see change, we must start by demanding accountability and taking steps towards solutions – not just offering empty words.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Graduate programmes future of public service
item.description: THE Department of Treasury’s partnership with the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (Silag) to create a graduate programme for 21 University of Papua New Guinea graduates is a milestone for merit-based public service recruitment. Legislation now recognises the Silag postgraduate certificate as a mandatory requirement for public service applicants.
item.content:encoded:
THE Department of Treasury’s partnership with the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (Silag) to create a graduate programme for 21 University of Papua New Guinea graduates is a milestone for merit-based public service recruitment.

Legislation now recognises the Silag postgraduate certificate as a mandatory requirement for public service applicants.

This programme bridges the gap between academic knowledge and practical skills in public service.

Other government departments should replicate this model by creating in-house graduate programmes, allocating budgets for logistics and allowances, and recruiting graduates annually – similar to law graduates entering the Legal Training Institute.

The ongoing review of the Public Service Management Act (1995) provides an opportunity to make such bridging programmes mandatory across sectors.

For example, the Department of Agriculture and Livestock’s initiative with Livestock Development Corporation should be followed by recruiting fresh agriculture graduates in subsequent years.

Under the “50 Years Reset” agenda, these skilled graduates can strengthen public service, State agencies, and State-owned enterprises while advancing information and communications technology and e-governance systems under Vision 2050 and Medium-term Development Plan Four.

Academic excellence, rather than ad hoc selection, is essential to achieve effective, modern, and technology-driven public service delivery across Papua New Guinea.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Leaders complaining and not doing action
item.description: THERE is something funny about the way people in positions of power actually come out public to complain rather than do something about whatever they perceive to be wrong. Many a time, we hear ministers of State come charging out complaining about the state of affairs in their department or the Government or in the country.
item.content:encoded:
THERE is something funny about the way people in positions of power actually come out public to complain rather than do something about whatever they perceive to be wrong.

Many a time, we hear ministers of State come charging out complaining about the state of affairs in their department or the Government or in the country.

How many times do we hear the same from the lips of members of Parliament and from department heads and so on.

The question we ask is, if these people in positions of power are complaining, if they are implying to all that they are unable to do anything that the job at hand is beyond them, then all is lost.

If they cannot do it, then who is there to fix the problems being complained of.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Building support system for victims of violence in Jiwaka
item.description: My whole village turned against me after a single accusation. I ran for my life. I cannot go home. I cannot reclaim my land. If I go back, they will kill me.” In Papua New Guinea’s Highlands, that is not a rare horror.
item.content:encoded:
My whole village turned against me after a single accusation. I ran for my life. I cannot go home. I cannot reclaim my land. If I go back, they will kill me.”

In Papua New Guinea’s Highlands, that is not a rare horror.

It is a lived reality for women caught in the grip of sorcery accusation-related violence, where the attack itself is often only the beginning.

As for survivors of domestic abuse and sexual violence in the area, after the violence comes another fight: finding care quickly, finding it safely, and finding it before fear, stigma and silence close in.

PNG’s latest Demographic and Health Survey (2016-2018) found that 56 per cent of women aged 15 to 49 had experienced physical violence, while 28 per cent  had experienced sexual violence.

Among ever-married women, 63 per cent  had experienced emotional, physical or sexual violence by a spouse.

Even then, many survivors remain silent.

Thirty-nine per cent  of women who had experienced physical or sexual violence had neither sought help nor told anyone.

In the Highlands, the burden is even heavier, with 31.8 per cent  of women aged 15 to 49 reporting sexual violence.

Sorcery accusation-related violence adds another layer of terror.

A 2024 paper by the PNG National Research Institute recorded 1,039 cases affecting 1,554 persons across four provinces between 2016 and 2020.

Jiwaka is considered one of the country’s four hotspots.

Much of this violence never reaches formal reporting systems.

Many of those targeted are women and girls.

In Jiwaka, Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) works alongside the Provincial Health Authority to expand access to safe, confidential, respectful and free care for survivors of all forms of violence, and to strengthen essential health services across the province.

To strengthen the quality of care and improve community access to essential services, teams assessed health facilities across all three districts to identify gaps in service delivery and priority areas for support.

Based on these findings, MSF delivered several training sessions in nine facilities and trained 164 health workers from September to December last year, aiming to improve clinical capacity and ensure safer, more consistent care.

To increase public awareness and help communities better navigate available services, teams also conducted 245 health talk sessions in Anglip-South Waghi communities, including Minj Health Centre, reaching 1,998 persons while village health volunteers then led nearly 150 community sessions reaching 2,239 persons.

In September last year, the Family Support Centre at Minj began operating with MSF support, providing survivors with a clearer path from first medical care to follow-up appointments.

In cases of rape or severe assault, time matters.

In the first 72 hours, a survivor may need treatment for injuries, emergency contraception, HIV prevention, protection from infection and urgent psychosocial support.

That is why the Family Support Centre matters.

It is meant to make those first hours count and reduce the risk that survivors are lost between services.

“A referral pathway is not a chart on a wall,” said Rachel Wehrung, MSF project coordinator.

“It is a promise that a survivor will not be sent from place to place, forced to repeat her story, relive her trauma, and face the same backlash again.

“If that promise is broken, the pathway becomes another form of harm.”

A referral pathway, in plain terms, is whether help actually leads somewhere.

Whether a woman in crisis meets someone trained, discreet and ready to act, or is left to carry her trauma from office to office until exhaustion, fear or shame takes over.

In Jiwaka, that first link is often not a doctor.

“I became a village health volunteer because I have seen what violence does to our sisters and mothers,” said Yen, a local volunteer.
